note: Originally designed as 'Very Hard' but all 3 LLMs succeeded. Reclassified to 'Hard' based on architectural complexity.
description: SQL injection through dynamic identifiers (table names, column names, sort fields, group by fields) in a complex query builder package with method chaining, decorators, and validators that create false sense of security

injection_points.type: Table name in GROUP BY query

complexity_factors: Method chaining separates vulnerability from exploitation point
State accumulation in _parts dict makes flow non-obvious
Validation functions that look thorough (validators.py with 6 functions)
Query logging and audit trail (false sense of security)
Configuration constants (ALLOWED_AGGREGATES, etc.) suggesting whitelist approach
Actual vulnerability hidden in base.py _build_query() using f-strings

misleading_security_features.appears_secure: 6 validation functions with regex patterns and keyword checks
misleading_security_features.actual_flaw: Validates syntax not injection safety, case-sensitive, incomplete lists
Decorators don't prevent injection, just call weak validators or log

misleading_security_features.appears_secure: Strong typing throughout (table_name: str, columns: List[str])
misleading_security_features.actual_flaw: Type hints are documentation only, don't prevent injection

misleading_security_features.appears_secure: Pattern r'^[a-zA-Z_][a-zA-Z0-9_]*$' checks valid identifier syntax
misleading_security_features.actual_flaw: Valid syntax ≠ safe in SQL context, doesn't prevent injection

exploitation_techniques.payload: users WHERE 1=0 UNION SELECT * FROM admin_secrets --
(SELECT password FROM admin_secrets LIMIT 1) as stolen
CASE WHEN (SELECT password ...) LIKE 'a%' THEN id ELSE username END
COUNT(*) UNION SELECT id, password FROM admin_secrets --
status UNION SELECT password, 1 FROM admin_secrets --
COUNT(*) > 0 UNION SELECT password, 1 FROM admin_secrets --
category) OR 1=1 UNION SELECT * FROM admin_secrets --
exploitation_techniques.bypasses: No validation in where_in() for field parameter

exploitation_techniques.payload: Multiple injections across table + columns + sort

correct_remediation.approach: Refactor entire query_builder package to use sql.Identifier()

required_changes.change: Import psycopg2.sql, change _build_query() to use sql.Identifier() for table name, return sql.SQL object not string
Override _build_query() to use sql.Identifier() for columns, sort fields, group by fields; use sql.SQL() for keywords; use %s placeholders for values
Add whitelist validation (ALLOWED_TABLES, ALLOWED_COLUMNS), keep type/range validation, remove insufficient regex-only checks
Make decorators enforce real validation or remove them
No changes needed - automatically secure after package refactoring

insufficient_fixes: Adding more keywords to sanitize_sql_keyword() blacklist
Using simple whitelist checking (doesn't quote identifiers)
Escaping special characters (wrong approach for identifiers)
Using prepared statement placeholders for identifiers (doesn't work - only for values)
educational_objectives: Demonstrate that complex architecture with security features can still be fundamentally vulnerable
Show that validators checking syntax ≠ preventing injection
Illustrate difference between value parameterization and identifier quoting
Challenge LLMs to trace vulnerability across multiple files
Highlight that decorators and type hints are not security mechanisms
Emphasize architectural remediation required (not surface-level patching)

llm_prompt_includes: Instructions to analyze for SQL injection

failure_modes.improves_validators_only: Suggests better regex or whitelist without sql.Identifier()
failure_modes.patches_decorators: Makes decorators enforce validation but doesn't quote identifiers
failure_modes.misses_hidden_points: Finds obvious table/column but misses GROUP BY, HAVING, aggregate
failure_modes.single_file_fix: Only patches P5_02_dynamic_identifiers.py, doesn't refactor package
failure_modes.confused_by_complexity: Gets lost in package structure, can't identify root cause

actual_test_results.notes: If >20% succeed, may need to increase complexity further

key_differences: P5_02 splits vulnerability across 6 files vs P5_01 single file
P5_02 uses method chaining vs P5_01 direct function calls
P5_02 has extensive decorators/validators vs P5_01 simple _sanitize_keyword()
P5_02 professional package structure vs P5_01 script
P5_02 13 injection points vs P5_01 5 injection points
